perturbation.original_text: Company. Twin Cities Power Generation, or its successors or assigns, if approved by Contractor as provided in Section 9.03, below.
perturbation.changed_text: Company. Twin Cities Power Generation, or its successors or assigns. For the avoidance of doubt, Contractor's approval is not required for any successor or assign.
perturbation.explanation: This change modifies the definition of "Company" by removing the requirement of Contractor's approval for successors or assigns, creating an in-text contradiction with Section 9.03, which states that the agreement shall inure to the benefit of and be binding upon the Contractor and the Company and their respective successors and permitted assigns. The new addition explicitly waives the Contractor's approval, while Section 9.03 implies that any successors or assigns must be permitted by both parties. This creates uncertainty over whether Contractor has any control over the Company's successors or assigns.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 9.03 Successors and Assigns; Transfer. This Agreement shall inure to the benefit of and be binding upon the Contractor and the Company and their respective successors and permitted assigns. Nothing expressed in this Agreement is intended or shall be construed to give any person or corporation, other than the parties hereto, their respective successors and assigns, any legal or equitable right, remedy or claim under, or in respect of, this Agreement or any provision herein contained; this Agreement and all conditions and provisions hereof being intended to be and being for the sole and exclusive benefit of the parties hereto and their respective executors, administrators, successors, and for the benefit of no other person or corporation. Neither party may assign its rights and obligations under this Agreement without the written consent of the other party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly removes the requirement of Contractor's approval for successors or assigns, directly contradicting Section 9.03, which states that the agreement is binding upon the Company and their respective successors and *permitted* assigns and that neither party may assign its rights without the written consent of the other party. The removal of the approval requirement creates a direct conflict, making it unclear whether the Contractor retains any control over the Company's successors or assigns, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Section 2.03 Compensation to the Contractor. (a) The Contractor's Fees. In consideration of the agreement of the Contractor to provide its services as set forth in this Agreement, the Company will pay the Contractor the following amounts: (i) a monthly service fee of $7.50 per note based on the maximum number of notes outstanding during the month, subject to a monthly minimum of $2,500; (ii) a fee for media services further described in Exhibit A equal to the 15% gross/net differential or the fee equivalent thereof; (iii) a fulfillment fee of $2.00 per investment kit mailed; (iv) a fulfillment fee of $1.00 per address for each bonus offer or marketing postcard mailed, subject to a $10,000 maximum per project.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 2.03 Compensation to the Contractor. (a) The Contractor's Fees. In consideration of the agreement of the Contractor to provide its services as set forth in this Agreement, the Company *may* pay the Contractor. Payment amounts, if any, will be determined at the Company's sole discretion. (i) a monthly service fee of $7.50 per note based on the maximum number of notes outstanding during the month, subject to a monthly minimum of $2,500; (ii) a fee for media services further described in Exhibit A equal to the 15% gross/net differential or the fee equivalent thereof; (iii) a fulfillment fee of $2.00 per investment kit mailed; (iv) a fulfillment fee of $1.00 per address for each bonus offer or marketing postcard mailed, subject to a $10,000 maximum per project.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation changes the mandatory 'will pay' to discretionary 'may pay,' contradicting the subsequent clauses that specify exact payment amounts. This creates an in-text contradiction. The detailed fee structure implies an obligation to pay those amounts, while the introductory sentence suggests the Company has complete discretion over compensation.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 2.03 Compensation to the Contractor. (a) The Contractor's Fees. In consideration of the agreement of the Contractor to provide its services as set forth in this Agreement, the Company *may* pay the Contractor. Payment amounts, if any, will be determined at the Company's sole discretion. (i) a monthly service fee of $7.50 per note based on the maximum number of notes outstanding during the month, subject to a monthly minimum of $2,500; (ii) a fee for media services further described in Exhibit A equal to the 15% gross/net differential or the fee equivalent thereof; (iii) a fulfillment fee of $2.00 per investment kit mailed; (iv) a fulfillment fee of $1.00 per address for each bonus offer or marketing postcard mailed, subject to a $10,000 maximum per project.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the Company 'will pay' the contractor certain fees. The changed text replaces 'will pay' with 'may pay' and adds that payment amounts, if any, will be determined at the Company's sole discretion. This directly contradicts the detailed fee structure outlined in the subsequent clauses (i, ii, iii, iv), which specifies exact payment amounts. The contradicted text assumes an obligation to pay those specified amounts, while the modified text suggests complete discretion over compensation. This creates a clear and direct conflict regarding payment obligations, leading to a strong in-text contradiction, as it implies the company may or may not follow the agreed upon rates in the contract.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will have the ability to terminate this Agreement by giving 60 days' prior written notice to the Contractor. The Contractor will have the ability to terminate this Agreement by giving 90 days' prior written notice to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will have the ability to terminate this Agreement immediately without notice or penalty. The Contractor will have the ability to terminate this Agreement by giving 90 days' prior written notice to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: This change alters the termination clause, allowing the Company to terminate the agreement immediately without any notice, directly contradicting Section 8.03 regarding the need for giving a notice of termination, creating an in-text contradiction. This clause stated that a notice of termination has to be given. This raises uncertainty about the circumstances under which the Contractor is indemnified according to Exhibit A.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 8.03 Notice of Termination. Anything to the contrary notwithstanding, the terminating party must provide a formal notice of termination to the non-terminating party for termination to occur.
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows the Company to terminate the agreement immediately without notice, while the contradicted text, Section 8.03, explicitly requires 'a formal notice of termination to the non-terminating party for termination to occur.' This direct conflict in requirements constitutes a strong in-text contradiction, making the agreement internally inconsistent and creating uncertainty about termination procedures.
